content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

January 17, 1996

Donald E. Bowling
Sierra County Board of Supervisors
District Number 4
Post Office Box 239
Downievile, California  95936

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-95-402

Dear Mr. Bowling:

     This is in response to your request for advice regarding your
responsibilities as a public official under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

     Does your familial relationship to your niece or employment
relationship with her parents prohibit you from participating in
land use decisions that affect the subdivision where your niece
resides?

CONCLUSION

     No, your familial relationship to your niece does not
prohibit you from participating in land use decisions affecting
the subdivision where your niece resides.  Also, your employment
relationship does not prohibit your participation provided the
decisions will have no material financial effect on your
employer's business.

_________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814  (916) 322-5660

File No. A-95-402
Page 2


FACTS

The Sierra County Board of Supervisors, of which you are a
member, will be considering the following land use ordinances
pertaining to lots within a housing subdivision located in Eastern
Sierra County: rezoning of lots from "GF" (General Forest Zone)
to R-1 and/or RH (Residential or Rural, respectively), adopting a
nondomestic animal maintenance overlay zone, adopting modification
to building ordinances for maintenance of
nondomestic animals, and possible mergers of adjacent lots to
increase lot acreage. There is a serious conflict within the
subdivision between those who maintain horses on their property
and those who do not.

Your brother's daughter maintains a horse on her property
which is within the subdivision. You have no economic
relationship with this niece or any of her immediate family and no
ownership or economic interest in any parcel of real property
within the subdivision issue.2 You do work part time for a
business establishment owned by your brother and his wife, but no
zoning decision by the Board of supervisors with respect to the
subdivision would have any economic effect on either your brother
or your brother's business for which you are employed.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in

_______________________
2 You mentioned in your request for advice that your niece and
"her family" own property in the subdivision. For the purposes of
this letter, I am assuming you mean your niece's spouse and
dependent children own the property, not her father and his wife,
who own the business for which you work part time.

File No. A-95-402
Page 3


the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for
a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by, or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(Section 87103.)

You may be disqualified if a decision will result in your
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of
your immediate family, increasing or decreasing by at least $250.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).) "Immediate family" is defined in
Section 82029 as the official's spouse and dependent children.
Because a public official's niece is not a member of the
official's immediate family for purposes of the Act, the familial
relationship between you and your niece does not, by itself,
create a conflict of interest for you.

You would be required to disqualify yourself from decisions
affecting your niece only if your niece has been a source of
income to you in the sum of $250 or more within the 12 months
preceding the decision. Any disqualifying income from your niece
would need to be from a source other than gifts because gifts from
your niece totalling $250 or more do not create a disqualifying
interest. (Section 82028(b)(3).) Consequently, absent some other
disqualifying financial interest as set forth in Section 87103,
you may participate in decisions concerning your niece's
subdivision.

The facts you have provided indicate that you do not have
real property, a business entity or source of income which will be
affected by the decision. (Section 87103(a), (b) and (c).) You
mentioned that you work for a business establishment owned by your
niece's father. The business is a disqualifying interest if you
are an employee of the business or if your part time income from
the business totalled $250 or more in the past 12 months.
(Section 87103(c) and (d).) As such, you may not participate in
any decision which will have a foreseeable material financial
effect on the business. You have indicated in your request that
the business will not be affected at all by the zoning decisions
regarding the subdivision. Assuming this is true, then the
decisions will not have a foreseeable, material financial effect
on your economic interest in the business and you may participate.

File No. A-95-402
Page 4


     If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        By: Liane Randolph
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LR:ak
